coredata.dc:title: Fluorometric and colorimetric detection of cerium(IV) ion using carbon dots and bathophenanthroline-disulfonate-ferrum(II) complex
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Sujie
Dai, Ruoyu
Hu, Yaoping
Han, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Cerium, an abundant lanthanide element, is widely used in human industry. The accumulation of Ce4+ ion, however, will damage the environment and biological organism. Therefore, its facile detection is highly needed. Herein, we design a hybrid sensing platform consisting of carbon dots (C-dots) and bathophenanthroline-disulfonate-Fe2+ complex (Bphen-Fe2+) for trace-level determination of Ce4+. Based on inner filter effect (IFE), the red-colored Bphen-Fe2+ complex severely quenches the fluorescence of C-dots. After addition of Ce4+, Fe2+ is oxidized to Fe3+, and the colorless Bphen-Fe3+ complex generates, which weakens the IFE efficiency and leads to the fluorescence recovery of C-dots. Meanwhile, due to the decreasing amount of Bphen-Fe2+ upon Ce4+ addition, the red color of the solution gradually fades, which enables visual detection of Ce4+ by the naked eyes. Under the optimized conditions, the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ system realizes the fluorometric and colorimetric sensing of Ce4+ in the range of 0.5–100 and 1.9–80 μM, with the limits of detection as low as 0.5 and 1.9 μM, respectively. This method also shows high selectivity over other common ions, and has an excellent applicability for monitoring of Ce4+ in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorometric and colorimetric detection
Ce4+ ion
Carbon dots
Bathophenanthroline-disulfonate-Fe2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Ce4+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2004), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2019A610188), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) XRD pattern, (c) Raman, (d) FTIR, (e) XPS scan, and (f) C1s spectra of the as-synthesized C-dots.
(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) PL spectra of aqueous C-dot solution. Insets in (a): photographs of aqueous C-dot solution in daylight and under 420 nm irradiation.

(a) PL spectrum of C-dots excited at 420 nm, and UV–vis absorption spectra of the Bphen-Fe2+ solution before and after addition of Ce4+ (Bphen-Fe2+/Ce4+). Insets in (a): photographs of the Bphen-Fe2+ solution before and after addition of Ce4+. (b) PL spectra of the solutions of C-dots, C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+, and C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+/Ce4+. (c) Zeta potentials of C-dots and C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+. (d) PL decay profiles of C-dots, C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+, and C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+/Ce4+.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the Bphen-Fe2+ complex in the different molar ratio of Bphen to Fe2+. (b) Effect of the mixing ratio of C-dots to Bphen-Fe2+ on the fluorescence recovery efficiency [(F–F0)/F0]. The concentrations of C-dots and Ce4+ are fixed at 8 mg L–1 and 100 μM, respectively. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ sensor in the absence and presence of Ce4+, respectively. (c) Effect of pH on the fluorescence recovery efficiency. (d) Effect of response time on the fluorescence recovery efficiency.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ sensor in the presence of different concentrations of Ce4+ (from bottom to top: 0, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 100, 120, 150, 200 μM). (b) The linear plot of fluorescence recovery efficiency [(F–F0)/F0] versus the concentration of Ce4+ (0–100 μM). (c) The absorption spectra of the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ sensor upon addition of Ce4+ at different concentrations (0–200 μM). (d) The linear plot of absorption declined ratio [(A0–A)/A0] versus the concentration of Ce4+ (0–80 μM). Insets in (c): photographs of the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ solution upon addition of different concentrations of Ce4+.

Selectivity of the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ sensor toward different ions, investigated by the fluorometric mode. Insets: photographs of the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ solution upon addition of different ions. The concentrations of all ions are 100 μM.
Schematic illustration of the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ sensor for detection of Ce4+.
Fluorometric determination of Ce4+ in real samples using the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ sensor (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorometric and colorimetric detection of cerium(IV) ion using carbon dots and bathophenanthroline-disulfonate-ferrum(II) complex

School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel hybrid sensor consisting of carbon dots and bathophenanthroline-disulfonate-
Fe2+ complex is constructed for fluorometric and colorimetric detection of Ce4+ ion.

Cerium, an abundant lanthanide element, is widely used in human industry. The accumulation of Ce4+ ion, however, will damage the environment and biological organism. Therefore, its facile detection is highly needed. Herein, we design a hybrid sensing platform consisting of carbon dots (C-dots) and bathophenanthroline-disulfonate-Fe2+ complex (Bphen-Fe2+) for trace-level determination of Ce4+. Based on inner filter effect (IFE), the red-colored Bphen-Fe2+ complex severely quenches the fluorescence of C-dots. After addition of Ce4+, Fe2+ is oxidized to Fe3+, and the colorless Bphen-Fe3+ complex generates, which weakens the IFE efficiency and leads to the fluorescence recovery of C-dots. Meanwhile, due to the decreasing amount of Bphen-Fe2+ upon Ce4+ addition, the red color of the solution gradually fades, which enables visual detection of Ce4+ by the naked eyes. Under the optimized conditions, the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ system realizes the fluorometric and colorimetric sensing of Ce4+ in the range of 0.5–100 and 1.9–80 μM, with the limits of detection as low as 0.5 and 1.9 μM, respectively. This method also shows high selectivity over other common ions, and has an excellent applicability for monitoring of Ce4+ in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Cerium, the most abundant element in lanthanide series, has been widely used in the fields of catalysis, biomedicine, electrochemistry, etc. [1–3]. Its ion, Ce4+, is a strong oxidant that can react with a variety of biomolecules including DNA, RNA, proteins and fatty acids [4]. A small amount of Ce4+ has good antibacterial properties, but its accumulation will seriously disturb gene expression and signal transduction, and damage cell membranes, organelles and nuclei [5,6]. The excess amount of Ce4+ in the human body can lead to thrombosis in the lung and liver [7]. Due to the destructive effect of Ce4+, it is really necessary to develop a facile method for its sensitive detection.

Fluorescence method has the great advantages of fast response, simple operation, and non-invasiveness. Over the past several years, different fluorescent probes have been applied for recognition of Ce4+ [8–20]. Huang et al. synthesized a rhodamine-based molecule bearing an N,N-dimethylaniline unit to detect Ce4+ through a metal-catalyzed oxidation mechanism [8]. Wang group prepared an organic framework for fluorescence detection of Ce4+ according to the principle of aggregated-induced-emission [9]. Thiosemicarbazide is also used for Ce4+ sensing based on the reaction of oxidative cyclization [10]. These cases are impressive. Nevertheless, the time-consuming and complicated synthetic procedures of the probes limit their wide applications. Thus, novel fluorescent materials with easy synthesis and excellent performance still need to be exploited for Ce4+ determination.

Carbon dots (C-dots), an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, have attracted considerable attention due to outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, cost-effective preparation, and easy functionalization [21,22]. These merits make C-dots promising sensors for detection of various metal ions, such as Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and so on [23–30]. However, few reports have focused on the use of C-dots in Ce4+ sensing.

In the present paper, we construct a novel hybrid sensor consisting of C-dots and bathophenanthroline-disulfonate-Fe2+ complex (Bphen-Fe2+) for sensitive and selective detection of Ce4+. The detection strategy is depicted in Scheme 1 . The red-colored Bphen-Fe2+ complex can remarkably quench the fluorescence of C-dots through inner filter effect (IFE). After addition of Ce4+, Fe2+ is oxidized to Fe3+, and the amount of Bphen-Fe2+ reduces, accompanied by the generation of the colorless Bphen-Fe3+, which weakens the efficiency of IFE and results in the fluorescence recovery of C-dots. Thus, both the fluorescence and absorption spectroscopy change with the increasing concentration of Ce4+. Based on this phenomenon, a hybrid sensor toward Ce4+ is set up.

Phenol, m-phenylenediamine, bathophenanthroline disulfonic acid disodium salt hydrate (Bphen), ferrous sulfate heptahydrate, and cerium sulfate were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Company. Other reagents were got from Sinopharm Chemical Company. All chemicals were used as received, without any further purification or distillation.

In a typical synthesis, 50 mg of phenol, 50 mg of m-phenylenediamine, and 30 mL of water were added in a 50-mL beaker. After 5-min stirring, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h to produce C-dots. For purification, the C-dot dispersion was filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane, followed by dialysis in a 1000-Da cellulose membrane for 72 h. Finally, the powder of C-dots was collected by vacuum distillation of the solvent.

For detection of Ce4+, the solution of citricacid (0.1 M) and Na2HPO4 (0.2 M) were mixed in a volume ratio of 3.9:1 to serve as a buffer (pH 3). In a typical run, 1 mL of a certain concentration of Ce4+ was added into 8 mL of the buffer containing C-dots (10 mg L–1) and Fe2+ (0.1 mM). After 15-min shaking for complete redox reaction between Ce4+ and Fe2+, 1 mL of the Bphen solution (1.6 mM) was introduced. The fluorescence spectrum of the mixture was recorded at the excitation of 420 nm. All tests were repeated for three times.

River and pool water were collected from the campus in Ningbo University. The real samples were centrifuged at 8,000 rpm for 5 min and filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane. Then, different concentrations of Ce4+ solution were spiked into the samples. Other experimental procedures were as same as described above.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by the JEM-2100 electron microscope at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data was collected on the Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Ká radiation (1.54056 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained on the Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer using the KBr medium. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was got from the AXIS ULTRA DLD spectrograph with Al/Ka as the source. Absorption spectra were obtained on the UV-3101PC spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on the Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. Absolute PL quantum yield was tested using the QE-2100 quantum efficiency measurement system. Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were taken on the Horiba FL3-111 spectrometer. Zeta potential data was measured by Zetasizer Nano-ZS90.

TEM image in Fig. 1 a shows that the as-synthesized C-dots are nearly spherical without apparent aggregation. Their diameters mainly distribute in the range of 10–17 nm with a mean value of about 14 nm (Fig. S1). The low contrast and indistinct lattice fringe of these dots indicate their amorphous nature. In the XRD pattern (Fig. 1b), C-dots exhibit two broad diffraction peaks at 25.0° and 45.0°. The former corresponds to the d-spacing of 0.36 nm, larger than that of 002 plane (0.34 nm) in the graphite, suggesting the presence of imperfectly arranged graphite-like structure [31]. The latter is close to the 100 plane of graphite. Such a peak is usually observed in the turbostratic carbon [32]. The poor crystallization of C-dots is also reflected in the Raman spectrum (Fig. 1c), where the disordered D band at 1362 cm−1 is stronger than the crystalline G band at 1569 cm−1 [33]. Fig. 1d shows the FTIR spectrum of C-dots. Several characteristic bands are presented, including the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H at 3429 cm−1, aromatic C = C/C = N groups at 1638 and 1619 cm−1, C–N bond at 1495 cm−1, C–H bonds at 1401 and 1384 cm−1, as well as C–O vibration at 1137 cm−1 [34,35]. The XPS scan spectrum (Fig. 1e) displays the predominant signals of C1s, N1s and O1s at 284, 399, and 533 eV, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) can be fitted into four peaks at 284.5, 285.2, 285.8 and 287.3 eV, corresponding to C–C/C = C, C–N, C–O and C = O groups, respectively [36,37]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. S2) shows the presence of C–N–C at 399.2 eV and N–H at 400.8 eV [38,39]. In the O1s spectrum (Fig. S3), two peaks at 530.6 and 533.0 eV are associated with the species of C = O and C–OH/C–O–C, respectively [40,41]. The FTIR and XPS interpretations prove that C-dots contain aromatic clusters in the core and bear plentiful hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl and amino on the surface.

The aqueous C-dot solution is orange in daylight (inset of Fig. 2 a). It has an obvious absorption peak at 288 nm that is related to the n–π* transitions of C = O/C = N groups [42]. An excitation-dependent PL behavior of C-dots is seen in Fig. 2b. The main emission band of C-dots is around 415–521 nm under the excitation of 320–480 nm. A bright sky-blue fluorescence can be clearly observed by the naked eyes with 420 nm excitation. The strong PL of C-dots may originate from the surface emissive trap sites [43]. Using an integrating sphere, the absolute PL quantum yield of carbon dots is measured to be 3.4%. The PL intensity of C-dots remains nearly constant at different ionic strengths (Fig. S4), which benefits their sensing application in complex environment.

The highly fluorescent C-dots are combined with Bphen-Fe2+ complex to serve as a hybrid sensor toward Ce4+. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the red-colored Bphen-Fe2+ complex has a strong absorption band centered at 535 nm, overlapping well with the emission peak of C-dots at 504 nm, which suggests that Bphen-Fe2+ can efficiently quench the fluorescence of C-dots due to inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [44]. Ce4+ ion, as a notable oxidant, can oxidize Fe2+ to Fe3+. After addition of Ce4+, the amount of Bphen-Fe2+ decrease, and Bphen-Fe3+ generates. The newly formed Bphen-Fe3+ complex is colorless, without any obvious absorption in the visible range. So, the IFE or FRET relationship is disturbed, and the fluorescence of C-dots recovers (Fig. 3b). For further investigation of quenching mechanism, the ζ-potential and PL decay measurements are carried out. Fig. 3c shows that both C-dots and Bphen-Fe2+ are negatively charged, with ζ-potentials of –3.72 and –9.47 mV, respectively, indicating the electrostatic repulsion. So, the distance between C-dots and Bphen-Fe2+ is larger than 10 nm, and the FRET process can not happen. Fig. 3d depicts the similar PL decay profiles of C-dots, C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+, and C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+/Ce4+. The fluorescence lifetimes of the three samples are approximately unchanged, all with values of about 3 ns. This fact further excludes the FRET mechanism and supports the IFE principle, because the fluorescence lifetime shortens noticeably in FTIR, but remains nearly constant in IFE [45].

To gain a good sensitivity, different detection parameters such as the molar ratio of Bphen to Fe2+, the mixing ratio of C-dots to Bphen-Fe2+, pH, and response time are systematically investigated. As displayed in Fig. 4 a, when the molar ratio of Bphen to Fe2+ is 2:1, the Bphen-Fe2+ complex has the strongest absorption, which can quench the fluorescence of C-dots in the maximum extent. Fig. 4b illustrates that at the fixed concentrations of C-dots (8 mg L–1) and Ce4+ (100 μM), the fluorescence recovery efficiency firstly increases and then decreases with the increase of the Bphen-Fe2+ concentration. The best result is obtained when the Bphen-Fe2+ concentration reaches 80 μM. The detection pH can influence the oxidizing ability of Ce4+ and affect the fluorescence recovery efficiency. Fig. 4c gives the optimal pH of 3. Fig. 4d shows that after addition of Ce4+, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ sensor gradually enhances with time and reaches a stable plateau at 15 min. So, the response time of 15 min is selected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Ce4+
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, different concentrations of Ce4+ are added into the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ solution. As seen in Fig. 5 a, with the increasing concentration of Ce4+ from 0 to 200 μM, the quenched fluorescence of C-dots gradually recovers. The fluorescence recovery ration [(F–F0)/F0] shows two nice linear relationships against the Ce4+ concentration over the ranges of 2–40 and 40–100 μM, giving the regression equations of (F–F0)/F0 = 0.0047 × Ce4+ (μM) – 0.0064 and (F–F0)/F0 = 0.0429 × Ce4+ (μM) – 1.6654, respectively (Fig. 5b). The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 0.5 μM based on the 3σ/s rule.

Due to the apparent color change from red to colorless, the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ sensor also enables the colorimetric determination of Ce4+. Fig. 5c depicts the absorption spectra of the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ solution in the presence of various concentrations of Ce4+. Two linear relationships between the absorption declined ratio [(A0–A)/A0] and the Ce4+ concentration are well calibrated in the ranges of 2–40 and 40–80 μM (Fig. 5d). The equations can be expressed as (A0–A)/A0 = 0.0037 × Ce4+ (μM) – 0.0080 and (A0–A)/A0 = 0.0158 × Ce4+ (μM) – 0.5112, respectively. The LOD is calculated be 1.9 μM.

The above results reveal that, in comparison with the colorimetric method for Ce4+ sensing, the fluorometric analysis achieves a wider detection range and a lower LOD. This is probably ascribed to the excellent optical properties and high chemical stability of C-dots.

To assess the specificity of the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ sensor toward Ce4+, a variety of common ions including Cl–, NO3 –, CO3 2–, SO4 2–, Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ are used as the potential interferents at the same conditions. These ions can not destroy the red-colored Bphen-Fe2+ complex. So, they cause negligible fluorescence recovery of C-dots (Fig. 6 ), which demonstrates the good selectivity of the sensor. It should be noted that, Cu2+ ion can bind with Bphen to from a colorless complex and affect the detection result to a certain extent. However, Cu2+ can be completely masked by EDTA. Cr2O7 2– and MnO4 – with strong oxidability can oxidize Fe2+ and facilitate the generation of Bphen-Fe3+ complex, leading to the fluorescence recovery of C-dots (Fig. S5). Fortunately, most water samples do not contain Cr2O7 2– and MnO4 –. Thus, the interference of the two anions can be ruled out.

Compared with other reported methods toward Ce4+ (Table S1) [9,11–16], the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ hybrid sensor realizes the fluorometric and colorimetric dual-readout modes, and offers high sensitivity and selectivity, providing a better performance for trace-level detection of Ce4+.

To evaluate the feasibility of the proposed method in actual test, the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ sensor is applied to detect Ce4+ in real water samples using the fluorometric mode. River and pond water are collected from the campus of Ningbo University, and spiked with Ce4+ at different concentrations. The results in Table 1 reveal that the recoveries of Ce4+ range from 91.57% to 97.28%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are below 8.26%. These data demonstrate a great potential of the developed sensor for reliable detection of Ce4+ in real samples.

In summary, by the combination of C-dots and Bphen-Fe2+ complex, a novel hybrid sensor has been developed for Ce4+ detection. Due to IFE, the fluorescence of C-dots is seriously quenched by Bphen-Fe2+. Ce4+ can oxidize Fe2+. The introduction of Ce4+ reduces the amount of Bphen-Fe2+ and disturbs the IFE relationship, leading to the fluorescence recovery of C-dots. In the meantime, the red color of the C-dots/Bphen-Fe2+ solution gradually fades with the increasing Ce4+ concentration. Therefore, the fluorometric and colorimetric analysis of Ce4+ is realized. This dual-readout method offers wide detection range, low LOD, and high selectivity, holding great potentials in monitoring harmful substances in the environment.

Sujie Han: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ruoyu Dai: Investigation. Yaoping Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Lei Han: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2004), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2019A610188), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120295.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation, characterization and molecular modeling studies of the beta-cyclodextrin inclusion complex with benzoguanamine and its analytical application as chemosensor for the selective sensing of Ce4+, Spectrochim
